speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Edward Mushal took place at his home in Potlatch on September 16th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'll start by asking you how your father wound up coming out here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, he was sent out here by the Weyerhaeuser.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I remember, couldn't I?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Potlatch Forest, it was the Weyerhaeuser then, see. He was sent out here from the east to grade lumber. See, there were several of them sent out here from the east, from Wisconsin, all over, to work in this mill when the first started here, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That's where all your lumbermen come from, back east from Wisconsin and Michigan and that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father was working for them back there at the time?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Yeah, he worked for the Weyerhaeuser back there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, those were just little mills back there in that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he care much about the idea of coming out here? Was he more into it?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, he wanted to come out here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I guess, I don't know, I was only five years old.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He must have wanted to come out here, he wouldn't have come out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the town there in?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: What was that town in Wisconsin that you... I don't know, just exactly what, he was from different places there. Marinette, Michigan, Menominee, Michigan, different places there, you know. Saxon, Wisconsin, all little mills were all around, you know, they moved from one place to another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he follow that lumber grading right along?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh yeah, that's all I've done ever since I came out here. Forty -four years down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You followed in his footsteps?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, there's nothing else to do here in this town then. This lumber business, that's all there is here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the first time you got here?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, of course when I quit school in the eighth grade and I went to work down here. Worked well with kids all during the summer months. We'd go to... We worked over there in the mill, cutting thistles and stuff like that around the yards, you know. Had a long handle rig to sharp point, plate like that. I'd just go along and dig these damming lines out. All over the yard and then the place else.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That's the first time you worked for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they pay the kids for doing that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, heck, I loaded lumber out there and yard for ten cents an hour, ten hours a day.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, ten cents an hour for ten hours. They don't believe that, by golly, that's what they were paying them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I worked for twelve and a half cents an hour in the mill, edging in the mill for twelve and a half cents an hour. Eight hours a day, sometimes ten hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about back in the Midwest? Do you know what your father used to make when he was working back there?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Not very much.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Heck, when he was only paying about ten cents here, there wasn't paying very much back there then. Because wages never were in the East like they are out here in the West.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He probably, I imagine, he probably made around thirty dollars a month and maybe a little more than that. But we were lucky to get that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Had your father graded lumber? Is that what he'd done for all his work and life? Was he into other, do other work too?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, that was all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: May I have his paper?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, that's all he knew. Well, of course he worked in a brewery back East for a while before that. But I don't know what, I think that was in Chicago where he worked there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Was he born in, uh, In Germany. He was born in Germany.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when he came over, about how old he was?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I don't know how many years a sister might know, but I don't know. It doesn't matter.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, I understand that they had pretty much cut out in the Midwest, the Weyerhaeuser, when they, by the time he'd come to Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know if that's the truth? He cut out? What do you mean?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, he already cut most of the timber, the good timber. Oh, heck no. They just started in here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, that first mill in Adams, John O 'Hare and Palouse.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I meant in the Midwest, not here. I figured they were just starting here. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, here in the West is where really, in the East is where the lumber business started. Then they kept moving out and they came out in the West here. That's where it really started, right out here. Because you take the middle of Montana, North Dakota, and all of them there, that's more farming, cattle country than the lumber business was out here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember coming out here when you were just a kid?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Yeah, we come here, got in the flue from Spokane. We got to Spokane, then we come down on the train to Palouse. And they had the train here and we come down here on the 10th of May.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Come here. We got down here, come in here about noon, 12 o 'clock, from Palouse down here at the depot. It had raining, boy, it was raining. Of course, no roads, old mud roads, you know. We walked with mud. Well, of course, we had those old shoes on, putting shoes in. And wading mud all the way from that depot clear to the back up here, those trees back where we used to live. All that old mud road, packing suitcases in that rain. No raincoats or anything, just the clothes we had on us. Boy, we were soaked when we come up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you bring much with you from the Midwest?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, just the suitcases, no.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: My dad had already been here and he got stuff for the house and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It worked that he came out first and got settled?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah, that mill, they put that up here in 1906 when they moved the mill up here. And then we come out here in 1908.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Now, there wasn't much of a town here then. Of course, they showed pictures of it, but like the whole town is right now, they said the whole town is already here. I don't know where they got the pictures, but they claimed that was in 1902. Well, the town wasn't even near in 1902. I don't know if anybody got that idea. Somebody had a picture on the calendar that the school puts out. Had a picture of the town. Said it was taken in 1902. Well, I don't know if I better because it wasn't the year of 1902. They couldn't very well build a mill here in 1902 and nobody living here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea of the company town? Did they just build the whole thing up?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah. They built these houses and then rented them out, see the people that work in here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they rented the houses out. Probably cost these houses, probably made a couple hundred dollars for this house here. And then you go paying $10 or $20 a month for it. It didn't take long to pay for it. Did that for years and years.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: So you can just see over 60 years they paid $20 a month for this house for 60 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think the company made money on this sort of thing?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They had to make money. You figure it out for yourself, 60 years is $20 a month. Almost said, well, it cost them about $200 to build it at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the maintenance, didn't they?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, they had to take care of it. They took care of all of it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, these houses weren't modern. None of them modern. All outside toilets and everything.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, the company, they put on this tar, old paper roofing and shingles, stuff like that. And they'd paint the houses, paper inside the house and that, wallpaper. They did that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That's all they did do. They had to do something to keep it up. Their houses, couldn't very well ask their renters to do it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Now if you wanted your house papered inside or anything, you'd just go to the office and tell them they'd come up and paper it for you. Pick out any kind of paper you wanted, wallpaper. Now we didn't have electric lights either, we had lamps. Most of the time, finally they put in electric lights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why would they want to own it all themselves and have to keep it up instead of just letting people buy it?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, they was making money off these houses. Wasn't costing them anything just for material paper and stuff like that. You was paying for that in rent. No, they didn't lose anything off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you start going to school as soon as you got here?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I started school at six years old here. I went to eighth grade in the equipment when I was in eighth and went to work. I didn't like school.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I've heard the school here was better than most places. Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, there's been more kids that graduated from this school here and got into better jobs than any other school around the country. They've always had good teachers here and everything here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, I can't think of his name now. He graduated from here and is one that designed that Lewiston Hill.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, there's several of them here that had good jobs.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Most kids around here had education. They went out and did pretty good so far.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I don't know, pilots, it seems that they put more money into the school here than any other schools do. Better teachers and everything. They pay more here than do in Moscow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the company in the early days that was running the school?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I don't know whether it was the company. No, I don't think it was.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: It must have been State or somebody because the company didn't keep it up.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, we didn't have to pay for our books or anything. All we had to pay for was pencils and paper and stuff like that. Our books were all furnished.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how old were you then? You were about 13 or 14 when you started working?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, let's see. I started at 6 and 8, 14, about 16 years old when I started working.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to get hired by then?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, all you had to do was during the summer go down and ask them for a job and they'd give it to you. I was always doing something, kids always had something to do. A lot of them, of course, worked for farmers and everything else. You'd go out in the summertime and pick potatoes. Farmers, they'd come along with a plow and plow the spuds up and we'd pick them 10 cents a sack.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, you wouldn't catch anybody doing that now. Go out in the field and pick the spuds for 10 cents a sack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was the first work that you did after pulling weeds for them?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: All the load went out in the yard and working out there and picking up lumber, scrapes a piece of lumber and cleaned up. And then loading lumber on trucks from the pile, put them on these four wheel trucks that they had. One man would be up on the pile and he'd hand the lumber down to the guy on the truck and he'd build it up and send the load.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Those loads would go with the planer, see?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: The surf was still lumbering everything there. Get up way up on those piles and then reach where sometimes you'd have to get up on the pile and you'd have to take two boards and hold your fingers this way. Then clear over the edge of the pile to get to the guy below could reach it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And then I went out and I went in through the saw mill. I worked in there picking edges and helping edgermen and edging and then the road carriage. Then I worked in the last mill and I worked in the fire hole during the Depression and then I went to the planer. Worked the re -till over there in a re -manufacturing plant and did away with that and I went back to the planer again and fed the machine there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Then I ran the re -saw.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I did about every kind of work there was around here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come you went from one time to work to another?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I thought a lot of guys... You're always looking for a better job to pay more, that's why.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Wherever you can get the most money is the job that you're working for. Because heck, if you were working for 80 cents an hour on one job and you got a chance to say 87 .5 cents or something, naturally you're going to try and get that job there. You just ask for a job, see. See that you wanted a certain job, maybe that guy that you wanted, they needed him on some other job, see. And then they transfer him and you get that job. You just put your application in for a certain job, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to the foreman and ask him?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you can do anything you want and then get a job then, but boy now why?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You take what you can get. If they want to put you on the job, you don't want to go where you're just out of a job, that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't that way then?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No,

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I quit the saw mill, the boss that they had there, and him and I didn't get a degree. I quit, I went over to the planer and asked the general manager over there for a job. He said, yeah, I said, I was just the man he was looking for. Sent me over to the replant there and that's all there was to it. I quit at noon and went to work right after that noon on the other job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you decide to quit?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I couldn't get along with that boss down there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He'd come along and he didn't like me and I didn't like him. And he's, I was edging in the mill, he'd come over there and he said, let me have your apron. I had a leather apron on. I handed him the apron, didn't say anything more. I went at noon and I just walked over and told the assistant boss there that he had, told him, I said, you better get another edgeman. I says, I'm through. That's all there was to it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: During the war, heck, I could have had a lot of women working down there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did she work down there? Which war?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Second World War. Yeah, she worked down there too. A lot of women working in there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: The remanufacturing plant where I worked by, they're putting all women working in there, except the graders and the guys running the trimmer and myself. The rest of them were all women pulling lumber and stuff in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that hard work for them to do?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, that's the life.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: When you get those two by 12, red fur two by 12s, 16, 18 feet long, and you pull one of those off the chain, that was pretty hard work for a woman to do. But I did her, a lot of times I'd go over and help them. I didn't have nothing to do. I'd help them pull lumber.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But they had to do it if they didn't do it or they were out of a job.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, take this boss that you didn't get along with. I've heard that some of the bosses were really pretty bad to work for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the matter with this boss? What was it that made him the man to work for?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He was a guy that, he was a barber in the first place, never knew anything about saw milling. He got in with a general manager here and the general manager would give him a job bossing down here. So he worked up here at Elk River for a little while up there in the saw mill, and so on. And I don't know how he got in with the general manager, but he got in anyway. He got to be a boss down there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Now the guy that was bossing there, he used to stay, my mother had to live down there. She had a few boarders, just guys come in and board and, you know, eating their meals. And one of these boarders was the boss then. So when this other guy came in and took his job, that made me mad too. That's when we didn't get along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To get to be a boss, did you have to have an in or did you do that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, that was the company you had, the general manager and superintendents, and that was the ones that picked your bosses.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Because those days, well you had to know your lumber business or something, you know, to get in here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But they had a lot of bosses. Now, heck, the kids that come out of school up here, high school, go down here and ask for a job and work here a couple of weeks. They put them in graders and bosses and everything else down there now. They don't have anybody working in here anymore in the lumber business. All the guys that do know anything about lumber all retired.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: All these kids up and down, I watched them here, oh, seven years ago through the planer. Kids grading down there and there wasn't grading lumber. They'd see a board come along and put a mark on it, pick up a knot and throw some other kid down there. They get by with anything down there now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they watch you pretty closely when you worked in the old days?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, you've got your life.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You didn't know what you was doing. If you didn't know what you was doing, well, you didn't get a job. You didn't fool around, play around then.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: The boss seeing you fooling around wasn't doing anything, just playing around. You've got command right now.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: All these kids down there now, the guys down there now, they get by with murder.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Some of the bosses they've got down there don't know anymore than the kids that are starting in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the bosses used to really know their stuff?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, they had to.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they had to know it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, I've heard about this easy fire, easy hire, easy fire, I've heard. It was easy to get on and the bosses could carry you for almost anything they wanted to.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, if you was even good with the boss, well, you were sitting pretty then. But there are a few of them that would get in with them.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Most of these guys are getting job bosses now, just to hold for the company, that's what they are. Guys that all run to the cotton boss or something, squeal on somebody else or something.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just like one guy here, a young fellow, working down here and he's a job steward for the union. Every night he'd run up to the office and tell them what the union is going to do and everything else, go to the meetings and tell them, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: This is narrow.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, he did that and by the way, he did that and finally put him in the boss down here. He's still the boss down there, yeah. Well, he may kick them out of the union.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, he'd run up and tell them. He was in the office all the time, what was going on, the union meetings and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, didn't they go for an awful long time? There was no union there?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, it was a long time.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They had a lot of international workers in America. And that was, men would come in, the AFVL would come in, the first IWWs and then the Silverman, the AFVL and then the CIO.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, you didn't have to go to the union, men, if you didn't want to.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: After you're done on there, work 30 days, I guess you have to sign up for the union now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the conditions like when you started working there in the mill? What were the conditions, the working conditions like? Oh, and what were you mean?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, you said it was a 10 hour day, instead of an 8.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about breaks and stuff like that? What about meals?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, well, they had their regular hours like starting at 7 o 'clock and then noon, and then at 2 hours at noon until 4 o 'clock. And from 1 o 'clock to 4 o 'clock. You didn't get no 10 minute break or anything like they'd do in the mill. And they didn't, you had to work straight through if you wanted to, if you wanted to smoke. You'd give some guy a ticket, or they'd say some ... wanted a toilet. Or even the claret, or even the toilet. It's called a ground toilet and smoke, they didn't do. That's the way the bird cat, you didn't get it. You wasn't supposed to be smoking. No.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You were coming in with some new service.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, we drove in with the reindeer.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: This is what looks so unreal to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, you gettin' this? You gettin' this paper? Yeah, well, when we got through with the paper, why would we let him have the paper?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Maybe he gets the radio and brings it down here, I said. I was in the middle of something. He was a ghost town. Every morning he gets the mail. He gets his mail when he brings the letters.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: All I remember is up in the corner, he owns us. Next to the wall is his fear.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I like the way I'm used to it. ... together, it was a good one.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, that was one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, that one.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, it's been over 40 years now.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, I remember all I remember was.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And we had one match here down in Palouse. Two brothers had come from Alberta, Canada. And Dewey fought one of them and I fought the other one. Down there in Palouse, we got five dollars apiece for it. Now, where they get thousands of dollars now, millionaires. We had to fight three rounds for five dollars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You each had to go three rounds? Did you go three rounds?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I busted my thumb there and I fought this guy.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: First thing I did, I went out and hooked him and I busted my thumb. I didn't have my hands wrapped or anything, you don't see. Just a glove.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Then, boy, you didn't get no 25, 50 dollars for about. So if you got 50 dollars, you'd have to fight about six rounds or maybe ten rounds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You busted your thumb when you first started right away?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, first thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You kept fighting him anyway?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, that's the area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You can still see it?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. I thought, Dolan manager there, I told him about it. Oh no, he said that's just a sprain. So I went out there and I just faked this guy all the time, see. Finally, in the third round, I came all along and hit him right in the stomach and I turned my hand in there. And boy, he just turned his stomach right upside down. Boy, you're not allowed to do that, you know. Boy, that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That's the only thing I could do. I had to do something.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I had a boxing school here for a long time too, for about a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, one kid over there. Well, I had about six or eight of them kids down here. I had a little boxing school, they wanted to learn the box. So I had to go out and I went to people and asked them to go on to donate. They wanted their kids in there, a box, you know, learn the box and that. Fine, but you go around and ask them for four bits or something to help buy gloves with them at. They didn't have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd go in the beer parlors and say, some guy would be sitting there, how about have his kid in there? And I'd say, how about donating four bits or something to buy some gloves or something?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I haven't got the money, but yet he had the money to sit there and buy beer all day long. And I'd go down here and get ready to buy a little radio, turn around and raffle that off. Get some money to buy gloves and I bought the gloves and ropes and everything else for these kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you charge your kids for a little?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I didn't charge them nothing.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Now that one kid over there and another one, this one here, he won the gold cup and the other one took the silver cup in the welterweight class.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Up in the tournament. Well, we fought guys from Washington, Canada and Sandpoint, St. Mary's, Spokane, all around the whole country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you go to?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: We went to the tournament up at Sandpoint.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you pick it up? Where did you learn how to box?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, there used to be a guy here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He was a professional fighter and his dad was a professional raster. Mel Mortison was his name. He came up here from Salt Lake City and he got a hold of me and I talked to him one time and he said, come on you guys, I want to try to go to boxing. So we went down there and he taught us how to box and so we kept it up. He had guys down there that were wrestling, boxing, everything else, but they had the old gym down here then. We had it down in the basement down there. And heck, they held boxes and matches and everything here then, that old gym. Now they don't have anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you and Dewey fought, was it just friendly?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, that was professional. When you fight amateur, you can't take any money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you fight against each other?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh no, no, we fought the other guy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, what the heck was his name?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Coach over at WSC.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Boxing coach. He came over here first before we went to WSC. He taught school up here and then he taught boxing here. And we put on a match down here one night. And he wanted me to box another guy and I went in there and this other guy was, oh he must have been about 6 foot 4, 5 inches tall, weighed 185 pounds and I weighed 132. I'm in there with that guy. Boy, all he does is stand out there and jab. I couldn't even get to him with very big long arms, you know. I stayed with him for 3 rounds and boy, I blackened my eye and bladed my nose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That doesn't sound like much of a match.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, what the heck was his name?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: From WSC. He was a boxing coach over there for years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever use that fighting for real? I mean, did you ever have to fight?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I've lost some.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Never asked for a month.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: One time, a guy over here at Onaway, the dancer over there, I went over and asked his wife for a dance. And he said, yes, I danced with him all at once. Somebody tapped me on the shoulder and turned me around and wham, let me have it. So after the dance, we went outside and asked him what the big idea was and both of us were drunk. So he told me, he said he didn't like the idea of me dancing with his wife. Well, I danced with him before a lot of times. So we started in there and the first thing he did was grab a hold of me and threw me down. Been raining muddy and a dickens over there. I just bought a brand new suit, a blue -sued, surge suit. He threw me down, started wrestling and I just got my hand under his arm this way. Screech on him, said I was choking him.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I said, I'll choke you, all right?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You get up and fight like a man. I said, I'll let loose. So he didn't say anything, just all he could do, holler, I'm choking you, you're choking me. So I says, all right. I says, you'll get up. I says, say you had enough, I'll let you up. He said, all right. He got up and quit.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That's what I used to do, heck, go to a dance to get in some old time.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were telling me about, um, you used to drive the band around?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that what you said? You drove the band around? And what's the story on that? How did it happen that you drove?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I just happened to have a car and these guys, these kids, guys, they organized an orchestra. There was five of them. They used to play all around the dances all around the country, you know. Not hauling around to these dances. They didn't have no other way of getting there, so.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They'd buy the gas and oil for me and not haul them around. I'd go to these dances anyway, so it didn't make any difference, you know. They had a pretty good orchestra then, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of stuff did they play? What kind of dances were they?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, everything. Waltzes and two -step and everything.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Other kind of jitterbug and all that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm sorry, better music than what they play now. That stuff they play now, I, uh, I'm kind of getting away from that, those what they call that bunch now.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Rock and roll. Rock and roll stuff. They're getting away from that now. You never hear of those guys anymore.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Because you couldn't understand what they were singing or anything else anyway. And that music they had, there was no music, just bum, bum, bum, bum. That's all they did. Couldn't dance by it or anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now, what, what were these dances like? Would it be just a little town to put one on? Everybody would come? What were they?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, that's what they do.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Some, let's say they put on a dance down here at this old gym down here. Well, they'd stick up posters around the country, you know, just dancing a certain night. People would come from all around the country, you know. Just like they do now. Of course, they don't put all these dances anymore now like they used to. Now, heck, every, about every Saturday night there's a dance, sometimes twice a week.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But now, all it is, people growing up don't go to dances anymore. Because it's all this, mostly kids and all the jitterbug stuff, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what, what towns would you go to from around here?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, all around here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: From here, Fluse and Deco, Garfield, Colfax. All around.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Genesee, Moscow, Genesee, Colton, Uniontown. All over. Oh, Pullman. They'd have these outdoor dances, you know, platforming, pavilion built outside. You know, dance out there lots of times. Over Moscow, Pullman, Colfax. I mean, in the summertime, sort of dancing inside the building was warm, they'd have these dance floors outside, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would it be mostly young people or would families go there?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, heck no.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No. Everyone, kids, young people and old people all mixed together there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, the young people, heck they would just waltz in everything just like old people would do. Grown ups.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you tell me that it was pretty easy to get a date when you went over there too?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Yeah, it isn't like now you go to a dance and see some woman there and go over and ask her for a dance and maybe she'd come there with somebody else or some other woman or something. Maybe ask her for a date, you know. Take her home. Lots of girls went to dances out of dates.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: People mixed more then than they do now. Heck, before I entered this town here, when everything was going good years ago, I don't know why. If somebody was sick with another town, why, you knew about it right away. You was up there helping them. Now you don't even know your neighbor across the street. People don't mix anymore like they used to. Now if anybody was sick or hurt or anything here in town then, why, everybody, the whole town went into helping.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now why?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You can't even trust the guys who don't know who your next door neighbor is or anything else. People don't care anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think it changed like that? What happened?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I don't know. People just don't seem to trust the other people anymore like they used to. So much of this rape and burning and stealing and everything else, breaking into homes. They never used to do that. Years ago you never had to lock your house or anything. You could go out and go on a vacation, come home, everything was just the same as you left it. Nobody tried to break in your house or steal or anything.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Because your neighbor across the street, boy, he'd be watching your place. You'd watch his or something. But the people didn't worry about anything then. Everyone trusted everyone.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But now, boy, you can't, don't know what to, who to trust or anything else now.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, you were talking about these dances before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the one thing you told me about, you saying that the band was really popular too with the girls, sort of like it is today?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they're all young guys, you know, these bands. Of course, these girls, they'd all go for these guys, you know, a lot of them.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, then, boy, if you could play an instrument of some kind, it was all right then. Of course, they had taught music in school, but it wasn't like they have the bands like they had now. The only music we taught there was mostly singing and stuff like that in school, see, choirs and stuff like that. But they'd have bands all right, but not very many. Kids didn't go, they didn't go much for school bands and stuff like that then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of, what do you say?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, like your basketball games and football games and that. They didn't have bands then, school bands or anything. Everybody turned out for their team people, all town, and everybody else turned out for your team, you know. They didn't have any bands and all that like they did now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there lots of drinking at those dances?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, but it wasn't like it is now, though. Those days were heck. Guys would be drinking and that, but they wasn't out fighting all the time and everything else, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, the kids couldn't buy anything either. The only thing you could get was beer and moonshine whiskey like that in those days.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You had to be 21 before you could even buy tobacco or anything else for smoking then.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I can't imagine that stopped young guys from smoking, though.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No. They'd always get some older guy to buy tobacco for them, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, they didn't pay much attention to it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: As far as drinking, you couldn't go into a pool hall or anything and buy, if you wasn't 21, you couldn't go in there and buy a glass of beer or anything. You had to be 21. Heck, they didn't argue about it or anything else then. Everyone knew you had to be that age and that's all there was to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story you told me about? Was it Jordan that stopped you on the road?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That time coming to Farmington. Some guy's been peddling liquor over there. That's when I took the orchestra over there, Farmington. And coming back, those guys bootlegging. They wanted to get away, see. They knew the cops were after them. And they went and turned and said it was me. Because I had that punch, see. And of course, naturally they stopped me on the road. And those guys turned me in and the ones that went the other way. They went over to Garfield and them come up here, come home. And I always took over the Woody Grade shortcuts, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Woody Grade?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Woody Grade. That's how I'd go out here by the highway out here and then cut across the country.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, they didn't. They figured that I turned and said I was the one. Because I had that orchestra holding them around, see. Those guys got away with it too. Some guys from here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what happened? You got stopped?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they just stopped us and went through the car and that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Looking for something, I told them, I said, ask them what they stopped for.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I said, you got liquor in your car?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And I said, no. Well, we told it you did have. Well, I says, you better take a look the other way. Then I said, the guys that you're after, they've gone another road. They didn't come over this way. I said, not only that, I says, the only reason they turned me in is because I had these guys as orchestra, see. Maybe they couldn't do anything about it then. Turned me loose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they ransacked your car?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Took seats all out. Jordan did. Took the seats out with the tools. And Summerfield made them put them all back too. Because they didn't have a warrant to search my car, see. But Jordan, he just went ahead and did that. I told him, I says, when Jordan, Summerfield said, well, come on, we'll go. I says, oh, no, you're not.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He says, why? I says, how about putting those tools, the seat and stuff back in the car? Summerfield says, what tools? I asked Jordan, he says, where'd those tools come from?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Jordan says, I put them there. He says, well, you put them back too, just the way you got them. He put the tools back in the car and the seats and everything and says, okay, you can go. I made Jordan mad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Happ Moody with them at that time?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But Happ Moody, he was clear over on the other end. He was watching for them to come out of the other way, going to Garfield, see. And those guys went by him all right. They was looking for mates because those guys turned me in, see. Told them off the track, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever talk to those guys about that, about giving them your name?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Yeah, they lived right over here at Onaway. I asked them what they had, and they just laughed and said, well, you see, he just wanted to get to do it, so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they tried very hard to catch these guys? I mean, was it a big, did they really try hard, these Moscow cops?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They'd go around to all these dances, you know, trying to pick guys up because it was all bootlegging then, you know. Heck, everybody that had a car, why, you was under suspicion.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: There wasn't very many around that had cars then.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And that's the way it used to be, around with a bunch like that, they'd go after you, you know. We were just one or two guys together that way, but they didn't pay much attention to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would you find out where there was moonshine to buy?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, there was always guys around. One guy knew, tells somebody else, you know, and you always knew some guy that was bootlegging. Heck, right here in town, there'd probably be three or four guys here in town. But you knew that they were doing it, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they would come right out in the open with it, but you had to catch them, though. Cops had to catch them, you know. Heck, you could go to a dance down here and go to one of those guys and he'd probably have bottles all over the, in ditches, covered up with a little dirt or something. You'd go along or under a sidewalk, wooden sidewalks, and pull out a bottle here and that and sell them, you know, find bottles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd sell them right on the street?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Right at the dance, if you wanted a bottle, I'd see one of these guys and they'd be right there at the dance and just tell them I want a bottle. Okay. Come on, go outside and get you one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the guys that bootlegged be the guys that made the moonshine or were they different?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, no, the other guys. Somebody else would make it, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Heck, those guys, there's one still down here by Palouse and then one by Pachini. And then there's another one out here in the country out here. They had three different ones that used to get around from here.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: One guy down here, Palouse, he had a little farm out there out of Palouse and boy, when he got through, he had a great big home out there and owned thousands of acres of land and everything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was all from moonshine?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Of course, he did a little farming too, you know, but...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the side?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And the cops raided that place of his, well, it was right out of Palouse, raided that place time after time and never could find it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I don't think they know today where he had it. You go out to his place there and there's only certain guys that could get it from him, you know. And they didn't even know the ones who got it. They didn't even know where he had it. They'd go out there to get it and you'd tell them, you wait. You'd have to stay at a certain place and he'd go out and he probably had it cast all around his farm, you know, all over jugs of it. But as far as the still, nobody ever knew where that was.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That sounds like a pretty good way to do business if you don't want to get caught, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But the guys that were selling, most of them got caught though. Cops would be watching for them all the time, you know. Because they'd go out and have bottles scattered all over, like going down the riverside to Kennedy for a dance. And those guys would have bottles spread all over, of course. Cops would be watching, see. They'd go out and get a bottle and they'd grab them right now.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Give them 30 days in jail, a hundred dollar fine or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was moonshiners the ones that got the big sentences if they got caught?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No. They never caught them. Well, they'd catch them but, you know, some of them didn't. Probably give them a month or two in jail and that's all in the fine.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They didn't go after guys just for drinking though. No.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: It's hard to believe that they'd have a law like that, you know, that nobody would pay any attention to. And they'd still have a law against it. Yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, they used to catch a lot of these cops who were in on it just as much as they bought off, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just like it is now, selling this dope and stuff, like over there in Moscow. You've got to get all the dope they want. Well, those cops, a lot of the cops were in on that if they were... You don't see them picking any of them up. Well, I guess they're getting down, so they've got a few of them down in Lewiston now in there. But I think that bunch over there in Moscow, I think those cops are all in on it, dope peddlers over there. They don't seem to do anything about it.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: In the same way here, they had a meeting here one time, the school superintendent. And he wanted to know where we could... I asked all the people to come to the meeting and wanted to know what we could do to clean up this town. And if they had any grievances of school or anything else. And we told him about kids getting, having dope and smoking in school and all this and that. Alright, he'd mark it down on the big board there, what they was going to do and everything. Come right down to it, they haven't did a darn thing about it. Who was meeting you going to tell them about the school and they never did a darn thing about it. I asked him, I says, well, you know, if these kids are smoking, he says, I know they're smoking up there at the school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how come you don't do anything about it?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, he says, you can't go around and tell every, see every kid, say now you quit smoking then.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He says, what good is it going to do?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He says, when your backs turn, they're going to smoke anyway.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He says, are you going to stop it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Poplites try to stop the booze from coming into the town?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I know they couldn't sell it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't let anybody sell it in town, right?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I mean, there was no, you couldn't buy it. There were no saloons in town.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But they had, there's Princeton up here, they had saloons there. Before when Potlatch was first built. Princeton was an older town than Potlatch is. And they had saloons and everything up there then. And guys would walk from here up the railroad track clear up to Princeton, get their stuff up there, you know, liquor and stuff and bring it home. Bring it home in gunny sacks.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That was a wide open town up there at Princeton, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Palouse used to be an open town too, years ago. Had saloons and everything there then too.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, I heard they had.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Palouse had the biggest red light district in the.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Colfax.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, Princeton was the same way. They had big dance halls and everything else up there, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, they used to get those horse and buggies and go from here, drive from here, clear down to Colfax. Horse and buggy.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Palouse. Well, they had a doctor down there in Palouse. And he used to drive up here with a team all the time. A hospital was down there in Palouse and he'd drive up here, you know, people were sick. And there was another doctor, LaParge, used to be over here at Onaway. He had an oxygen team that he used to drive. Come into town here. He had his office over there. And then they had, after that, why they brought in two doctors here. But they, then he died over there. He was a good doctor too, an old fellow. Now I finger there. I was out here one time when I was a kid and I come up the street up here, a bunch of us playing. I jumped over a bank over there and dined a piece of glass, a bottle or something. And I cut my finger there. And I didn't pay any attention to it, it fester up in that. My golly, it all turned blue and everything. So, I went over to that doctor over there in the part and he looked at it and said, Well, I was a kid then, he says, Oh, I'm going to have to cut that finger off. He said, No, you're not. I started out, all out of his office. He laughed and he says, Come on. He took that finger and he opened it up there with his festered. And I soaked that, I had to go over every day and soak that for two hours. And some blue medicine that he put in a pan. And I had to sit there and hold that hand in there for two hours. And I brought that finger up there in the scar or anything else on that finger now. But poison, it said, blood poison, it said in it, you know. Well, he saved that old finger in there. Now, heck with it, it happened. Now you go to a doctor and he'd probably cut the finger off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of that other doctor that was here in town that was a surgeon? Oh, Gibson?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Gibson. Gibson Thompson. They're the ones that was in the hospital here. And then there was Dr. Duprey. He left here and he went over to Moscow. And he got killed in an accident coming from Pullman. And he's all in. One of his doctors would get up in the night and work day and night, you know, traveling around. Somebody was sick, he'd get up. And he was all in and come back from Pullman one morning. I guess he went to sleep or something and went over in the ditch and killed him. He was a good doctor, too.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Can you tell me that Dr. Gibson, was it Dr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Gibson you set a patient's bone for?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. He set his leg.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, he broke his leg. I guess it was down in here or up in here or something. And he set it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it happen that he asked you to do it, though?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, the nurses were all busy, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He had to have some help. And I was just walking around there after the operation I had. And he just asked me to help. Because there's nobody else there. Of course, after he set it, then one of the nurses happened to come up there. He, of course, he told them to get that stuff ready for him, you know, put a cast on. Well, when the nurses was doing that, then he set the legs, see. And then when he set it, then this nurse come up and wrapped it all up and then put that plaster stuff on there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, that didn't bother me at all.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: It bothered me to do something like that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, my dad got hurt down here, got killed down here. Had his head split right open that way. Brained everything all the way out, head wide open. I helped the doctor down here put a badminton's head up and I helped him do that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, he got hit with a switch engine down here. They had a switch engine down here. The engine was just coasting along. They used to have warehouses down here. And they were close together. Had a platform across there, across the track. And he was just walking between these two warehouses there and just as he stepped on the track, this engine come along and hit him. Knocked him clear over to what they had, a sheep trail there. Knocked him over into that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Where they were supposed to be ringing a bell or blowing a whistle, he could come across there, across from there. They didn't do it. They were just coasting along, see. And he didn't hear them. Just as he stepped in, it was hitting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He didn't die right away, though?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, that happened around noon and he didn't die until about 12 o 'clock that night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think you had much of a chance to go home?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No. No. His head split open. His part of his nose cut off there and that head splits open. His brain is all hanging out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did the doctor do?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, put it back in just so he was up. That's all he could do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, when you get hit that way, why?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: There ain't much they can do for you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he conscious? Hmm?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, no.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, he never again conscious.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, when your brain quits functioning that way, why?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And actually there's nothing to work for in your heart, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just like if he was in the car wreck and got hit on the head or something, oxygen conscious. A lot of people are injured inside but never again conscious. They live for a little while and then they're gone, you know. So it must work on your brain. Your brain works on your heart, too, you know. It has to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that it was the company's fault?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No railroad. The guy that was running the engine wasn't supposed to be in there. He was a brakeman. He was just, got in there just to learn to run it, see, and he had no business in there. He was sitting up there in the cab, you know, and the engineer let him run it, see.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, there was a guy seeing the whole thing there but I went down to find him while he was gone. The company got him out of there right now because the company owned the railroad, see. House Company Railroad, then WI&M. Washington, Idaho and Montana, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what do you mean he was gone? They took him out of potlatch?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, because heck, if we'd have got a hold of him, we could have sued the company for everything we wanted.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, these guys that was in the train, they seen this guy sitting there, see, and knew that he'd seen it. Because one day after they hit him, they naturally, this guy hollered at him, stopped the engine, I guess. Of course, then they found out who, they, naturally, the company when they asked about it, well, they told them about this guy being there and they knew him, see. And they got him out of town right now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You never got to speak to him at all?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, couldn't even find out who the guy was.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: None of the men who were on the train would, could... Oh, no, they wouldn't say anything because they were afraid of their jobs. They wouldn't say anything.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But there's one guy that happened to be down there at that time walking down. There happened to be there right after this had happened, see. And he talked to this guy that was there. And he's the one that told us about it, see. Of course, naturally, he told me, I run down there to see that guy and they got him out of there. He's gone, man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You never sued the company then? Or, I mean, the railroad?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, my mother didn't want to sue because at that time, if you'd sued the company, they'd have kicked us out of town, see. So my mother just took a... They offered her a couple thousand dollars and she took that and that was all due. Because she didn't want to leave here because she'd been here all the time since the town was built, you know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: She didn't want to leave here because she knew everybody here and didn't know anybody else.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, the company, they could do just as they pleased then, you know, in those days. That's why.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You sued the company for anything, boy, you had to get out of town. Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember about when it was that that happened?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No. Let's see, is it... 72 and 20.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: About 50, 51 years ago.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, I was 21 then when that happened. 51 years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know of other deaths in the mill, other people dying besides your father?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, there's been lots of hurt down here and that, you know, killed.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: One little Swede got run over with... They had these electric motors, battery motors, pulled loads out of the yard on tracks, you know. Hooked up on these trucks, see, hauled lumber out. He went to kick a switch. Got his foot caught in the switch and this other guy ran right over his leg and cut his leg off. He died, he bled to death. By the time they got him up in the mill, they had to bring him up in a wagon then, horses. They got him up to the office before the hospital built him. Where the library is now, there used to be a little building there, doctors in there. And they brought him up and by the time they got him up there, well, he bled to death. Couldn't do anything for him.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Another guy was oiling down here at the mill and saw a mill and lumber down into a green chain. He crawled under the green chain there to oil it, gears and stuff under there. And his clothing got caught some way and he just dug him right in there and chewed him right up. Nothing left, just ground him right up. They went in there and dug him out. I helped dig him out there with shovels, scraped shovels and pulled his body out that way. He was just all ground up. Caught up in his gears and just kept going around, you know. Nobody knew he was under there until finally somebody came by there, I guess, or something. And seeing stuff flying out, they ran up and stopped the saw mill. Stopped the chain and that. And they went over there and pulled him out. He got a big piece of canvas and just put his body, just took it out and put it on this canvas. That's all there was to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you use? What kind of shovels?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just a great big scoop shovels. The only thing you could get out there with.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, when they buried him, they didn't bury nothing there. Just ground up stuff. And you could see it's a part of his hands and legs and stuff like that, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the men tried to be real careful? Did they think about safety a lot in them early days?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh no.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, they didn't go much for safety then. That was up to you if you seen where you could get hurt or something. You'd stay away from that job, that's all. Of course, if a boss seen you going into some place where you wasn't supposed to do something where you could get hurt, he'd tell you about it, you know. Tell you not to do that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But it was pretty hard those days, why heck. You wasn't thinking about getting hurt, you were just sitting there doing your work and that was all.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You'd take on the saw mill.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Ever in the mill saw mill?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You know, these big bandsaws they had on the carriages where they cut the lumber logs. A lot of times those were great big saws that break. Boy, those steel would just fly all over there. No wonder nobody ever did get hurt on that though. A lot of times the goods come in there and the carriage would be just making the cut and probably get about halfway through a log and when those saws would break, boy, that old steel would just fly all over, that old saw would fly all over. Guys were lucky that way then. Never did get a man hurt. I've had lots of times when I was running the resaw, had lots of saws break. Just fly around inside there. Just lucky that I wasn't very close to them. Well, I was close to them all right, just right by them, but of course they were all covered up, you know, and just happened to hit on the inside of these covers. All you hear is one big bang and all you could do is just run away from it and then after the figures all broke up and you go over and push the button and stop it. Of course you didn't have time to stop it when you heard it break. Boy, you had to get out of there. A lot of times I've had them break and fly all over down there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You just ran?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, it's the only thing you could do, just get away from it. Then you had figures walk up there and see that you wouldn't get hurt. Well, you can go up and stop it. A lot of times you could hear the saw just break about halfway through. You could hear that clicking. Naturally, you just push your button and stop it, you know, and just step back a little ways so in case it did break all open you wouldn't be there. No, I've had lots of them break that way.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just like that finger there running the novelty saw. And darn the sliver got caught on the saw. I took a laugh and went to push it in there to get that sliver away from that saw. And I stopped the machine and I hadn't completely stopped, just barely moving. And that laugh broke and I threw my hand in and just chewed that finger up in this one here. I cut this one about half off and this one here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It broke and drew your hand in?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, I just threw my hand in there. See, it had a little wheel there with little cogs on it that pulled the lumber into the saw. Well, that laugh got caught on that little cog wheel and that threw my hand in. See, it just numbed it right away. Of course, when I pulled my hand out I could see that glove, that finger just dropped down in the glove there. Just took my glove off and threw it down the ground, cussed a little bit, put my fingers in my hand this way. Went over to the boss and told him they took me off the electricians there and took me in a pickup and brought me up to the hospital up here. It was in the wintertime, it was in December, cold, old man, it was cold. And he brought me up there and called the doctor and he came down. Old Dr. Gibson, I don't know what happened, I told him. He said, all right, lay down there on the table. Laid there on the table and put my arm out that way and shot a needle in there and fingers. And I just sat there and watched him and he just bandaged them up, sewed them up and cut this one off there and watched the whole thing there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And then after I'm done, I come home here. I just come home and I just sat down in Davenport. Oh, blood just started coming all over me, bandages. He told me to stay there and I said no. So when I went back, he said, well, I told you, you'd be back in about five minutes. And so he said no, I had to soak my hand then, took the bandages off and had to soak my hand, some stuff there. Sat there in the chair when he got through, he bandaged it all up again. And now upstairs for you, I had to go upstairs and stay there in the hospital overnight. And next morning I got up and I come home again. Because there's no use sitting right there in the hospital, there's nothing you can do anyway. I had to go out in there and have it bandaged up again. I was eight weeks of that darn thing there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: A lot of times now, I'll get a hold of something and my belly just slips right out. Forget about it, usually where you have a good hand, you grip on something, this one here, you forget about that finger being off of there and it slips right out of your hand.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I never noticed it, even when you were starting to tell me.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, a lot of people, I'd write a letter and people say they don't find out I happened to take my hand away and that finger goes out that way. You only got four fingers there. I knew a kid that used to be here, he had five fingers, two little fingers. One growing right off the other side of the other one. Five fingers. I've seen him with toes, five toes.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But you know, I don't know why you don't see that anymore, people growing that way. I don't know what caused that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get any compensation for that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you pay for that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, the company had insurance and they paid you so much a finger in that part, see. They still do yet. They paid you so much a finger, it all depends on what finger it is. Now that was the ring finger, see. They pay so much for that, so much for little finger, ring finger, and then so much for the number of fingers you lose or your hand. It's all got different, you get different prices for it, you know. I got paid for that little finger there, the same as if it was cut off. Because Gibson told me, he said, I'll sew that on there and that will give you a finger there. And he says, you get paid for it just the same as if that finger was off.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Because it's no use, you can't do nothing with it, see. Still, it gives me a better look at it. If you had to cut that off, I'd only have those fingers there then, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you think the company cared a lot about the men that worked for them?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they really try to look out for the men?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: It sounds like when your father died, they just tried to look out for themselves and not for your family.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Well, they knew we could sue them, see, if we had that guy there. Because when they found out who was running that engine line, naturally they were in the wrong.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And that's why they'd have to do something because heck, we could have sued them for millions there for that.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But when you go fighting a corporation, boy, you've got to have the money or like us being here, a mother and that, in a town like this where they could tell you to get out and the company, town, what could you do?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You couldn't do anything.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: But now, like this here thing that happened to somebody down here, why?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: The company couldn't do it if they can't say, well, get out of town. They couldn't do it. They'd probably make it tough for you that they'd keep putting you from one job to another until they find some fault with you, candy or something, you know. But now it's different. They can't kick you out of town like they could before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there people that they kicked out of town in the early days?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Nobody that you know ever got kicked out?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, the only time was during the war.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They kicked all the Japs out of here during World War II. That was the only thing.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I didn't rush them right out of here or anything. They just told them to leave, that's all. Nobody did anything to them or anything. They just let them move right out of here. As long as they got out of town, that's all the people cared about.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Most of them, some of them went left here and went to Spokane, put in concentration camps and like that, you know. Some of them, younger ones, they went right back to Japan to fight, you know, to where they sent them right out of the country, you know, shipped them right out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You thought that the Japanese were alright though, right?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh yeah, they didn't bother anybody here. There's some of them that left here and went to Spokane and they kept them up there. I don't know if they were here for years and years. They just never left Spokane, stayed right there. Some of them went into business up there.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, we had a druggist down here, a little Japanese. He couldn't ask for a better guy than he was. He was married, kids, nice kids. He lives up here right out of Spokane now. And she worked with him down here when he was a druggist and she worked in the drug store. Heck, every Christmas he sends us Christmas cards and everything else. Writes to us every once in a while.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, he's well liked wherever he goes. He's a good druggist.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is he still in the business?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. He's out of Spokane or some place. I don't know, just where he is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that time you told me that this guy got into a fight with the Japs and the Mill?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, that George Alexander. Him and his brother, they were working on the edgers. George and his brother's edging on one edger and then the Jap and his brother was on the other rig. And it was both side by side. And old George, of course, he didn't like the Japs and then started fighting with them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about that George?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He's a guy that I don't know of. When I was sick he tried to be nice to me. Before I was working down at the Mill, he's a guy that always tried to do something against somebody all the time. My wife was working down there, she tried to make it hard for her. But now he's fine and dandy, you know, whether he's conscious bothers him or what.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I wonder why a guy would be like that. Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: To have some kind of a meeting, he's always got to get his nickel's worth in, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? You said that you stopped him from hurting those Japs that time?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, he was going to push them through the edger and I jumped over there and grabbed a hold of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't he know what he was doing?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: George, sure, he knew. Whether he was trying to scare the Jap or what, whether he really meant it, I don't know. I just happened to be right on the side of him, about from here at the wall over there. I wasn't that near the chair. I just jumped right over a table there at Rose and grabbed a hold of him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How would George think I'm almost getting canned over that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Him and his brother both.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Japanese guy?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: He must have been scared to death. Oh, he was scared to death.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he do anything at all that caused that?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, George and his brother just had it in for him. I just didn't like the Japs.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That was before the war broke out. Of course, right after that, that's when they made the Japs leave then. They all had to quit then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever get to know any of the Japanese that were here working in the mill?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, I never had anything to do with them.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: After the war broke out, of course, these two there, they left and went back to Japan. They joined the Army over there, of course. I asked different ones. I knew a couple of them. They were up there in Spokane. I asked them about them. They said they went back to Japan. They didn't know whether they were killed in the war over there or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go in their boarding house down here?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, I was trying to sell raincoats and shoes and stuff on the sidelines. I'd go down there and they'd order stuff from me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go to Japan?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah, I didn't bother anything. Of course, there were only a couple of them that could talk any broken English anyway. They couldn't understand. You'd have to show them the stuff and explain to them. They'd show them the shoes or the raincoat and ask them how size they wore and stuff like that. No, but I'd go down there and they'd all order stuff from me. Get the stuff and bring it back down there to them and they'd pay me for it. They didn't argue and say, well, I think what I ordered or this and that. They'd take it and say nothing.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I didn't say anything with the Greeks. They used to be Italians. Sell them stuff too that way. Make a couple of dollars on the side.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you notice much differences between the Greeks and Italians?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah. They never mixed very much together.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: How the Greeks stay on their side of the street over there. There's two rows of houses there and the Greeks would be on the side and the Italians would live on the other side. Some of them down at the other end of town. They didn't mix together very much.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Of course, they all worked together down at the mills matching all those.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: That gun won't win.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yesterday, I liked a roast to death. It was hot over in Moscow yesterday. Around 90, sitting out here on the front porch all day.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: I can't take that wind out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think in those early days that the Greeks and Italians were a very different kind of people from each other? Or do you think they were pretty similar in the way they were?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: They all lived about the same kind of life.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: There wasn't much difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the Greeks do a lot of drinking too, like Italians?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Italians too. They'd wine all the time.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And then bootlegging, those Italians would do a lot of bootlegging with wine. Wine all the time. So, wine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did either group mix much with the townspeople? Did either of the Greeks or Italians mix much with the townspeople?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Yeah. Whenever there was anything going on there, everybody mixed together. It didn't make any difference with the Greek or Italian or what you were.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: No, everybody was the same around here. You couldn't very well hold against you, relate what you were.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Just like in World War I. My dad was a German. And there were some Canadians here. Well, they of course, they'd come down here and they'd stand around. They wouldn't work or anything else. And they hollered up my dad one time and tried to stop him downtown. And they were standing up against the hotel down there, the old building. And they stopped him and told him to get out of town. He was a German. He was just a little guy, over five foot tall.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And they told him to get out of town. He told him, he said, I don't have to go any place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And he said, why don't you go back to the county? He said, how come you're not fighting? He said, why don't you get out and have a uniform?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You're not even working down here, standing around here, waiting for people to feed you. Of course, my dad, heck, he bought bonds and everything else, you know, and that. Nobody ever said anything to him. And he had a garden. He grew a squash. Oh, great big thing might come out of that. And he gave that to Red Cross and they'd raffle that off and they got $500 out of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of his squash?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: Uh -huh.

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: People bid on it, you know, different ones. They got $500 on that. And donating, you know, people come in, they'd bid. Well, I'll say, you went in, I'll bid $5.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All right?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: You'd give that $5. That was the donation. That was your bid. And then somebody else would bid, well, I'll give $6 or $7. Well, whatever they could, they'd bid on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, whatever they bid is that amount they'd have to donate, see?

speaker: Edward Mushal
words: And they got $500 out of it that way, out of that squash.

speaker: Edward Mushal
